The surgeon performs skeletonization of the base of the pediatric appendix, revealing the tinea coli and the base of the appendix near the ileocecal junction, ensuring complete dissection up to the cecum.

The surgeon performs skeletonization of the base of the appendix, carefully dissecting to reach the cecum near the tinea coli. The tinea coli and base of the appendix are visible, with the ileocecal junction located below.

The surgeon continues skeletonization of the appendix using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the teflon jaw is positioned towards the appendix to prevent perforation. The base of the appendix is nearly reached.

The surgeon ties the appendix using Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot technique, while performing irrigation and suction to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon ties the appendix using Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot with a configuration of multiple hitches, winds, and locks. A darker reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip is used to prevent tissue crushing and suture breakage during the knot application.

The surgeon demonstrates the application of Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot with a configuration of one hitch, one wind, one lock, followed by additional winds and locks. A darker reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip is used to prevent tissue crushing and suture breakage.
The surgeon positions the tip of the Vandarkar knot pusher at the base of the appendix, successfully ligating the appendix. The knot pusher is then removed, and the suture is cut.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the appendix approximately five millimeters away from the knot, effectively burning the mucosa and sealing the distal end to prevent contamination. A five millimeter telescope is then utilized for visualization.

A 5mm telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa, while a 10mm umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdominal cavity. The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo bag to prepare for specimen retrieval.

A ten millimeter umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdominal cavity. The endo bag is visible entering through the umbilical port, while a five millimeter telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa. The mouth of the endo bag is opened inside the abdomen.

The surgeon unfolds the endo bag and creates space inside it using an instrument. The appendix is placed into the endo bag and extracted through the umbilical port. The umbilical port is closed using a cobbler's needle, ensuring all layers except the skin are sutured.
